bibliographic.citation: The Romaunt of the Rose: From the Unique Glasgow MS, Parallel with its Original Le Roman de la Rose, edited by Max Kaluza, Part I—The Texts, Chaucer Society Publications, First Series 83 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Chaucer Society, 1891).
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Chaucer Society
bibliographic.title: The Romaunt of the Rose: From the Unique Glasgow MS, Parallel with its Original Le Roman de la Rose

entities.description: Bilingual parallel edition with the Middle English text on odd-numbered pages and the Old French source on facing even-numbered pages. Kaluza used Hunter 409 for the extant English text, Thynne's 1532 print for lost and locally deficient passages, and earlier editions for cited corrections and variants.
entities.label: Max Kaluza, The Romaunt of the Rose, Part I—The Texts (1891)
notes: In the cached PDF, the main title page is PDF p. 10, the part title is p. 14, English printed pp. 3–391 are on PDF pp. 16, 18, …, 404, and English printed pp. 393–439 are on PDF pp. 408, 410, …, 454. Two publisher-circular pages intervene at PDF pp. 406–407.
U-M encodes the discontiguous English odd pages and excludes the facing French even pages.

rights.basis: The 1891 publication is beyond the ordinary U.S. copyright term; U-M marks its source materials public domain and Internet Archive metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the U.S.
rights.component: 1891 editorial text, typography, and underlying medieval texts
notes: This copyright conclusion is separate from conditions attached to a particular provider's scan.

dateStatements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century; other Glasgow pages describe c. 1440–1450

entities.description: The sole near-complete multi-folio manuscript witness of the Middle English Romaunt, copied in England by one principal scribe with corrections or additions by a second hand and later marginalia. The poem now begins imperfectly at line 45 on f. 1r and ends at line 7692 on f. 150r; eleven leaves are reported lost.

holding.notes: William Hunter collection; rebound by S. M. Cockerell and Son in 1963.

entities.label: University of Glasgow Library MS Hunter 409

physicalDescription.extent: The public folio sequence exposes fols. 1r–151v and 152r; the Romaunt occupies current fols. 1r–150r
notes: Hybrid anglicana formata script in black ink; one principal scribe, with second-hand corrections or additions and later marginalia.
Eleven lost leaves are stated in Kaluza's headnote; Glasgow's summary bullets describe the losses inconsistently and require reconciliation.
Lines 7109–7158 and 7159 are displaced relative to lines 7013–7302, an order also reflected in Thynne's print.
physicalDescription.support: Support not verified from the consulted public institutional descriptions

entities.description: The first collected Chaucer print edited by William Thynne. Its Romaunt text is manuscript-dependent and is Kaluza's source for passages absent or locally deficient in Hunter 409.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1532 and is beyond the ordinary U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: 1532 printed text and underlying medieval work

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is the extant Middle English translation/adaptation, not the French Roman de la Rose, Hunter 409 as a physical object, Kaluza's bilingual volume, or the U-M encoding.

Fragment A (lines 1–1705), Fragment B (1706–5810), and Fragment C (5811–7696) are durable scholarly attribution units, not divisions visibly imposed by Hunter 409; the manuscript presents a continuous poem.

The phrase 'unique Glasgow manuscript' is inherited from Kaluza and the Glasgow project. Hunter 409 is the sole near-complete multi-folio witness, but a detached Romaunt leaf in NLS Acc.10225 is a second fragmentary witness.

A cached manuscript bundle marked complete is complete for Glasgow's published sequence of fols. 1r–151v and 152r. The provider exposes no covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, edges, or f. 152v and no larger public profile was found.
Textual lineation, physical foliation, printed pagination, PDF capture pages, and provider sequence positions are recorded as separate locator systems.

lineage.summary: CME00038 is the General Corpus XML representation of the Middle English Romaunt of the Rose alone. The University of Michigan encoding derives from Max Kaluza's 1891 bilingual parallel edition but excludes its interleaved Old French column. Kaluza transcribed the extant English text principally from University of Glasgow Library MS Hunter 409 (V.3.7), supplied the manuscript's lost passages from William Thynne's 1532 print, and recorded corrections or variants from earlier editions. This graph distinguishes the intellectual work from its manuscript witnesses, printed editions, electronic encoding, repository artifact, and digital facsimiles. The complete 303-image folio sequence publicly exposed for Hunter 409 and a complete scan of Kaluza's volume are cached; the limits and provider terms of each surrogate are explicit.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer (Fragment A, almost certainly); anonymous or disputed translators (Fragments B–C)
catalogSummary.date: Fragment A often dated to the 1360s and before 1372; Fragments B–C undated here; first printed in 1532
catalogSummary.region: England; Fragment B has Northern English dialect features
catalogSummary.title: The Romaunt of the Rose

catalogingSubject.label: The Romaunt of the Rose
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is the extant Middle English verse translation/adaptation traditionally called The Romaunt of the Rose. It is transmitted as a continuous poem but comprises three durable scholarly attribution units produced by different or disputed translators. It excludes the French Roman de la Rose, the Hunter 409 codex as physical object, Kaluza's bilingual edition and apparatus, the U-M encoding, and repository XML.

contentParts.label: Fragment A

locators.value: line 45 through line 1705, with additional internal loss at lines 333–380

notes: Lines 1–44 and 333–380 are absent from current Hunter 409 and are supplied by Kaluza from Thynne; smaller defects also occur near lines 45–48, 69–72, 94–96, and 117–120.
The part boundary is scholarly and attributional, not a visible manuscript division.
contentParts.summary: Traditional lines 1–1705, opening the dream framework, walled garden, fountain and rose quest; now almost certainly attributed to Chaucer.

contentParts.label: Fragment B
notes: The NLS leaf overlaps the Hunter lacuna at lines 2403–2442 and also preserves lines 2443–2450, which survive in Hunter 409; its exact component identifier and date remain unresolved.
The part boundary is scholarly and attributional, not a visible manuscript division.
contentParts.summary: Traditional lines 1706–5810, a continuation by an anonymous translator whose forms and rhymes show Northern English linguistic features.

contentParts.label: Fragment C

locators.value: lines 5811–7692, with loss at 7383–7574 and a displaced sequence around 7013–7302

notes: The traditional endpoint 7696 does not map without qualification to Hunter 409's terminal line 7692 or to every terminal alternative represented in Kaluza's apparatus and the XML.
The part boundary is scholarly and attributional, not a visible manuscript division.
contentParts.summary: Traditional lines 5811–7696, a continuation whose authorship remains disputed; some scholars accept Chaucer's responsibility and others reject it.

assertion.notes: No numeric range is normalized because the institutional summary presents a debated scholarly placement, not a securely bounded event.

dateStatements.display: often placed in the 1360s and before 1372, but debated

notes: Publication date is distinct from translation, manuscript copying, and modern edition dates.

scope.description: First printed appearance in Thynne's collected Chaucer

extent.description: One continuous verse work represented through three scholarly attribution fragments. The XML contains 7,712 L elements and 199 LG elements in total; ancestor-aware counting yields 7,696 main-stream verse lines in 196 main-stream line groups, with 16 additional L elements in three apparatus notes. Traditional fragment line ranges total 7,696. Hunter 409 itself ends at manuscript line 7692, so terminal manuscript, edition, and encoding lineation remain distinct.

formStatements.description: Continuous Middle English narrative verse, predominantly in octosyllabic rhyming couplets.

genres.label: Translation and adaptation
notes: The three fragments translate and adapt portions of the French Roman de la Rose; the relation does not imply uniform literalness or common authorship.

genres.label: Social satire
notes: Satirical force is especially prominent in the poem's social, clerical, and religious critique rather than equally dominant in every passage.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Old French is the language of the related source work and Kaluza's excluded facing column; it is not assigned as a language of the cataloged CME00038 content.
The three fragments are modeled as attribution and linguistic parts of one continuously transmitted composite work, not as three visibly separated manuscript works.

Geoffrey Chaucer is not assigned blanket authorship of the whole poem. Fragment A receives the strong modern consensus attribution; Fragment C records both an unidentified translator and the disputed Chaucer alternative.
Guillaume de Lorris and Jean de Meun belong to the related French source-work layer and are not assigned direct authorship of the Middle English Romaunt.

William Thynne and Max Kaluza are edition-layer agents. Kaluza appears in the catalog summary because CME00038 is represented through his edition; Thynne's supplied passages and all edition ancestry remain explicit in the linked lineage graph.
The eleven leaves missing from current Hunter 409 are physical-witness losses, not missing content in the represented work: Kaluza supplies their principal line ranges from Thynne's 1532 print.
Title pages and the closing notice of Troilus and Criseyde belong to the source representation; they do not turn Kaluza's volume or the following work into the cataloging subject.

next_steps: Review current stylometric, linguistic, textual, and source studies for both fragments.
Record alternative author and date claims with claim-specific confidence rather than assigning one whole-work author or date.
openQuestions.question: Who translated Fragments B and C, is Chaucer responsible for any of Fragment C, and when were those fragments composed or translated?

next_steps: Consult recent Chaucer chronology and source studies.
Keep translation date distinct from witness copying and first publication.
openQuestions.question: What date range for Fragment A is supported by current scholarship beyond the traditional 1360s or pre-1372 placement?

next_steps: Collate Hunter f. 150r, Kaluza's final English pages, milestones, line elements, and nested terminal notes.

notes: The work belongs to the English Middle English translation tradition; this does not prove that all three fragments were composed in one location.
Dialect evidence is not converted into an exact place-of-composition assertion.

assertion.notes: The modern edition page characterizes the current consensus as almost certain rather than absolutely proved.

notes: Translation includes adaptive treatment of the French source; 'translator' does not imply word-for-word rendering.
Northern forms and rhymes distinguish this translator's language from Fragment A; they are not treated as proof of a precise place of composition.

assertion.notes: The translator's identity is unresolved; this responsibility records the unknown agent rather than choosing between competing attributions.
Some scholarship accepts Chaucer's authorship of Fragment C and other scholarship rejects it; this is an alternative disputed attribution, not a second translator claim.

scope.description: Possible responsibility for Fragment C, traditional lines 5811–7696

notes: This is editorial responsibility for a source edition, not authorship of the medieval intellectual work.

scope.description: The 1891 printed source representation used by U-M

notes: Kaluza used Hunter 409, Thynne's 1532 print, prior English editions, Michel's French edition, and other French manuscripts in distinct editorial roles documented in the lineage manifest.

scope.description: The 1891 English and parallel French texts
Especially the satirical continuation in Fragment C

summaries.value: A Middle English dream-vision translation and adaptation of part of Guillaume de Lorris and Jean de Meun's Roman de la Rose. A dreaming narrator enters a walled garden populated by allegorical figures, falls in love with a rose, receives instruction and opposition in his pursuit, and encounters extended reflection and satire concerning love, social conduct, and religious hypocrisy. The extant English poem is composite: Fragment A is now almost certainly attributed to Chaucer, Fragment B is anonymous and linguistically Northern, and Fragment C remains disputed.

CME00038 encodes only the Middle English text selected from Kaluza's 1891 bilingual edition. Kaluza's facing Old French column, the edition as a physical publication, Hunter 409 and the NLS leaf as witnesses, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML are related or lineage layers rather than parts of the cataloged intellectual work.

The A/B/C divisions are retained because they remain stable units for attribution, date, and dialect. They are not described as original manuscript sections: Hunter 409 presents a continuous poem, and Olivia Robinson argues that its mise-en-page does not support reifying the scholarly fragmentation.

notes: The opening is absent from current Hunter 409 and is supplied in Kaluza's text from Thynne's 1532 print; it is an incipit of the represented work, not a surviving Hunter 409 incipit.
The XML's final trailer derives from the printed representation and continues with notice of Troilus and Criseyde; that following work is outside CME00038.
titles.value: Here endeth the Romaunt of the Rose

work.author: Geoffrey Chaucer (Fragment A, almost certainly); anonymous or disputed translators (Fragments B–C)
work.dateDisplay: Fragment A often dated to the 1360s and before 1372; Fragments B–C undated here; first printed in 1532
work.regionDisplay: England; Fragment B has Northern English dialect features

A dreaming narrator enters a walled garden populated by allegorical figures, falls in love with a rose, receives instruction and opposition in his pursuit, and encounters extended reflection and satire concerning love, social conduct, and religious hypocrisy. The extant English poem is composite: Fragment A is now almost certainly attributed to Chaucer, Fragment B is anonymous and linguistically Northern, and Fragment C remains disputed.
